FULL CONTENT:
![Home](https://www.sil.org/sites/default/files/sil_logo_glyph.png)

# Why care about endangered languages?

![](/sites/default/files/inline-images/philipzurenuhelpingstudent_-_smaller.jpg)Why is language endangerment a serious concern? The language that is spoken in the home while children are growing up expresses a people's culture, values, thoughts and world view. Loss of language is almost always accompanied by social and cultural disruptions. When a language disappears, the intangible heritage of all of human society is diminished.

![](/sites/default/files/inline-images/philipzurenuhelpingstudent_-_smaller.jpg)

Concern about language endangerment is centered on the factors which motivate speakers to abandon their language and the social and psychological consequences of language death for the community.

Those concerned about language endangerment recognize the implications for communities first and foremost. In addition, loss of linguistic diversity also impacts our knowledge of language and communications as a human phenomenon.

[<<  BACK TO ENDANGERED LANGUAGES](/about/endangered-languages)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Political turmoil often forces communities to abandon their languages for survival, leading to loss of cultural identity and language transmission.
Globalization promotes dominant languages, pressuring communities to adopt them for economic opportunities, marginalizing local languages and cultures.
Societal stigma around minority languages can discourage their use, leading to diminished intergenerational transmission and eventual language loss.
Migration often results in language shift, where communities adopt the dominant language of their new environment, risking the extinction of their native tongues.
Insufficient governmental or educational support for minority languages leads to inadequate resources for teaching and preserving these languages, accelerating endangerment.
